title: Run a Stage 4 Science Fair
overview: Bring together everything from Stage 4 Science to run a science fair that teaches visitors the most important ideas from this stage through real investigations.

project.drivingQuestion: How can we use the science from Stage 4 to run an accurate, safe and exciting science fair for our school or community?
project.finalProduct: Create a science-fair plan with a live investigation, labelled displays, a safety checklist, a visitor survey with a data display, and a short explanation of your scientific decisions.

stages.title: 1. Foundations display
stages.prompt: Choose the two most important ideas from Units 1, 2. Build a display that teaches each idea with a model or diagram, an example and a check question.
stages.evidence: Two labelled displays with models, examples and check questions

stages.title: 2. Live investigation
stages.prompt: Pick one investigation from Units 3, 4 and run it live: state your hypothesis, follow the method, record results and explain your conclusion.
stages.evidence: A completed investigation with hypothesis, method, results and conclusion

stages.title: 3. Connections wall
stages.prompt: Create a connections wall that links ideas from Units 5, 6 to everyday life, with at least three labelled connections.
stages.evidence: A connections wall with three labelled links to daily life

stages.title: 4. Present and explain
stages.prompt: Present your science fair. Explain at least three scientific choices, check that your conclusions match your evidence and identify one improvement you would make.
stages.evidence: Spoken, written or recorded scientific explanation

evidenceChecklist: A safety checklist for every activity
A connections wall with three links
A survey with organised data display

rubric.secure: Observations, measurements, models and conclusions are accurate and checked.
Hypotheses, fair testing, recording and conclusions follow the scientific method.
Labels, diagrams, tables or charts make the science visible.
Decisions are explained using appropriate science words and evidence.

questions.question: An animal with a backbone is called a:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 1 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: vertebrate. A vertebrate is an animal with a backbone, or spine.

questions.question: Which of these is an invertebrate?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 1 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: ant. An ant has no backbone, so it is an invertebrate. The others are all vertebrates.

questions.question: Energy is best described as:
options: the ability to do work or cause change
questions.answer: the ability to do work or cause change
questions.hint: Use the Unit 2 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: the ability to do work or cause change. This is the scientific meaning of energy.

questions.question: Which type of energy does a running goat have?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 2 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: kinetic energy. Anything that moves, like a running goat, has kinetic (movement) energy.

questions.question: Which of these is a state of matter?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 3 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: liquid Solid, liquid and gas are the three states of matter. Metal and glass are materials; hardness is a property.

questions.question: All materials are made of:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 3 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: tiny particles Every material is made of tiny particles far too small to see.

questions.question: The thin, solid outer layer of the Earth where we live is called the:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 4 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: crust The crust is the thin, solid outer rocky layer we live on.

questions.question: The very hot centre of the Earth is made mostly of:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 4 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: iron and nickel The core is made of these two metals, not rock.

questions.question: We can see objects that do not make their own light because:
options: light reflects off them into our eyes;
questions.answer: light reflects off them into our eyes;
questions.hint: Use the Unit 5 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: Light reflects off objects into our eyes. Our eyes do not send out beams; they catch light.

questions.question: Which of these is a natural light source?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 5 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: The Sun is natural. Torches and bulbs are made by people, so they are artificial. A mirror is not a source at all.

questions.question: Electricity is a kind of:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 6 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: energy Electricity is a form of energy that can flow and change into light, heat, movement, and sound.

questions.question: A complete circuit must be:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 6 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: an unbroken loop Current only flows if the path is a complete loop with no gaps.
